title: Jesus Christ, God and Man
html:
The Great Question

What happened when the Author stepped into His own story?

Imagine you're writing a novel. You've created a world, characters, conflicts, plotlines. You know every detail because it all came from you. But your characters are in trouble. They've rebelled against the story you gave them. They've wrecked the plot with their bad decisions. And you decide to do something unthinkable: enter your own novel. Become one of your own characters. Walk your own streets. Feel the cold you invented. Suffer at the hands of the villains you created. Die at the hands of your own characters. And rise again, rewriting the ending from the inside.

That — exactly that — is what happened in a forgotten corner of the Roman Empire, in a stable in Bethlehem, two thousand years ago. The Author of all reality — the Word through whom everything was made, as John says — stepped into His own creation. Not as a divine tourist. Not as an actor disguised as a human. He entered for real: He ate our food, felt our weariness, wept our tears, and carried our sins. The Infinite became finite. The Eternal entered time. God became man.

Think of the most famous person in all of human history. There is no debate: it is Jesus of Nazareth. The years are counted from His birth. Billions of people have dedicated their lives to following Him. And yet we don't have a single physical description of Him in the Gospels. We don't know what His face looked like, His height, the color of His eyes. No paintings, no statues from His lifetime. Why? Because what matters about Jesus isn't what He looked like — it's WHO HE IS. And that question — the most important ever asked — is the one He Himself poses to us: "But who do you say that I am?" (Mk 8:29).

The Catechism of the Catholic Church devotes sixty paragraphs (CCC 422–483) to answering this question. And it does so without half-measures: Jesus of Nazareth is true God and true man. He is not just a great teacher. Not just another prophet. Not an enlightened sage. He is God Himself who became one of us. And this mystery, which the Church calls the Incarnation, is the center of our faith. Because if Jesus is only a teacher, we can admire Him; but if He is God made man, we must follow Him, worship Him, and entrust our entire lives to Him.

Activity

Imagine you're playing a video game, and suddenly the creator of the game appears as a character inside their own creation. They don't come with cheats or flying: they start at level 1, just like you. They learn the rules, take the hits, face the same bosses. That's what God did when He became human.

Draw two circles: one with the word "GOD" and another with "MAN." In Jesus, both circles become one. Write "JESUS" inside the combined circle. Ask yourself: if Jesus is truly God, how does that change everything He said and did?

1. The Name that Says It All: Jesus, God Saves

The Catechism begins Christology with a truth so simple it can escape us: "Jesus" means in Hebrew "God saves" (CCC 430). This isn't just any name chosen by Mary and Joseph. It is the name God Himself reveals through the angel: "You shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins" (Mt 1:21). The name already contains the entire mission of His life.

In the ancient world, the name revealed a person's deepest identity. It wasn't a label: it was a declaration of who you are and why you exist. When God directly gives a name — as with John the Baptist ("God is merciful") or with Jesus Himself — that name is a life's program. Jesus didn't come to give lectures, or to found an NGO, or to propose a healthy lifestyle. He came to save. And not from a political, economic, or social problem: from sin, which is the root of all evil (CCC 430–435).

But the Catechism doesn't stop at the name. Jesus receives multiple titles, each illuminating an aspect of His mystery (CCC 436–455):

  • Christ (Greek) / Messiah (Hebrew): meaning "Anointed One." Jesus is the One anointed by the Holy Spirit, consecrated for the mission of salvation. He is not a political messiah as many expected, but the Messiah who fulfills all of God's promises: king, prophet, and priest (CCC 436–440).
  • Lord (Kyrios): a title that in the Greek translation of the Old Testament was reserved for God Himself. By calling Jesus "Lord," the first Christians confessed His divinity. "No one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit" (1 Cor 12:3) (CCC 446–451).
  • Son of God: a title given in the Old Testament to angels, the chosen people, or kings. But on Jesus' lips it means something radically new: He is the only, eternal Son, who knows the Father as no one else does (Mt 11:27). The centurion at the foot of the cross — a pagan — confesses it: "Truly this man was the Son of God" (Mk 15:39) (CCC 441–445).
  • Son of Man: Jesus' favorite way of referring to Himself. It evokes the vision of Daniel 7:13–14, where a "son of man" receives eternal dominion from the Ancient of Days. But Jesus gives it a disconcerting twist: the Son of Man comes not in glory, but to serve and give his life as a ransom for many (Mk 10:45) (CCC 440).
  • Word (Logos): the most theological title, used by John. Jesus is the eternal Word of God, the divine Reason through whom everything was created. "In the beginning was the Word... and the Word was God" (Jn 1:1). And then, the unthinkable: "And the Word became flesh" (Jn 1:14) (CCC 422–423).
  • Emmanuel: "God with us" (Mt 1:23). The name the prophet Isaiah announced eight centuries before. God doesn't save us from afar: He becomes one of us.
  • Lamb of God: John the Baptist points to Jesus with this title (Jn 1:36), evoking both the Passover lamb and the Suffering Servant of Isaiah who "like a lamb was led to the slaughter" (Is 53:7) (CCC 608).

Each name is a window into the same mystery. And they all point in the same direction: Jesus is no ordinary man. He is God with a human face.

Context

To say "Jesus is God" is the most scandalous statement one can make. The Jews of Jesus' time knew exactly what it meant: either it was blasphemy, or it was the greatest truth ever uttered. That's why, when Jesus said "Before Abraham was, I AM" (Jn 8:58) — using the very name of God revealed to Moses at the burning bush — they picked up stones to stone Him. There was no misunderstanding: they understood perfectly. Either Jesus was God, or He deserved to die as a blasphemer.

C.S. Lewis, the great British writer and convert to Christianity, posed the famous trilemma: Jesus claimed to be God. If He wasn't but believed it, He was a lunatic — on the same level as someone who thinks they're a teapot. If He wasn't and knew it but said it anyway, He was a diabolical liar. But if He really was God, then He is the Lord. There is no fourth option. You can't say "Jesus was a great moral teacher" and then ignore that He claimed to be God. A great moral teacher doesn't claim to be God unless He actually is. Lunatic, liar, or Lord. You choose.

2. True God: The Eternal Son of the Father

Jesus is not just a man especially close to God. He is not an enlightened being, an avatar, or an intermediary between God and humanity. He is God Himself made man. John's prologue says it with a clarity that leaves no room for vague interpretations:

"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. (...) And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth." (Jn 1:1, 14)

The Word — the Son — existed from all eternity. He was not created. He is God, consubstantial with the Father. And that eternal Word "became flesh": He entered our history, our time, our biology. The Creator became a creature without ceasing to be the Creator (CCC 422–423).

Nicaea: The Battle for the Divinity of Christ

In the year 318, a priest from Alexandria named Arius began preaching something that sounded very reasonable: Jesus was the first and most perfect creature of God, superior to the angels, but not God Himself. He was "of a substance similar" to the Father (homoiousios), but not of the same substance. Arius had a catchy slogan: "There was a time when the Son was not."

The entire Church entered a crisis. This wasn't a theological discussion for specialists: salvation itself was at stake. For if Jesus is not true God, then on the cross God didn't save us: a creature saved us, however exalted. And a creature cannot save us; only God can reconcile humanity with Himself. The Emperor Constantine convened the first Ecumenical Council at Nicaea (325 AD). More than three hundred bishops — many bearing the scars of the recent persecutions — defined that Jesus is "God from God, Light from Light, true God from true God, begotten, not made, consubstantial with the Father (homoousios)" (CCC 464–465).

It wasn't an easy victory. Arianism survived for decades, supported by emperors and court bishops. But the faith of the Church prevailed. And every Sunday, when we pray the Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed, we repeat those same words, forged in the battle for the most important truth in the world.

Jesus Himself left no doubt. To Philip, who asks: "Show us the Father, and it is enough for us," Jesus replies: "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father" (Jn 14:9). And in another moment He declares: "I and the Father are one" (Jn 10:30). This is not a metaphor. It is not mystical poetry. It is a real identity: in Jesus, God Himself — everything that God is — comes to meet us with a human face (CCC 466).

Deepening

Catholic Christology developed through the first four Ecumenical Councils, each responding to a different error:

Nicaea (325): The Son is true God, of the same substance as the Father. Against Arius.

Constantinople I (381): The Holy Spirit is also true God. The Nicene-Constantinopolitan Creed is completed.

Ephesus (431): Mary is Theotokos (Mother of God), because the One born of her is God. Against Nestorius, who separated the two natures to the point of practically denying the Incarnation.

Chalcedon (451): Jesus has two complete natures — divine and human — united in a single Person "without confusion, without change, without division, without separation." This formula of Chalcedon is the definitive definition of the hypostatic union (CCC 467).

Recommended reading: St. Athanasius, On the Incarnation of the Word. One of the most accessible and luminous patristic texts on why God became man. Also C.S. Lewis, Mere Christianity (chapter on the trilemma), and YouCat 47–55.

3. True Man: Like Us in All Things but Sin

But Jesus is not only God. He is also truly man. Not the appearance of a man, not a divine disguise, not God "dressed up" as a human being. He is a real man: born of a woman like any baby, raised in an insignificant village, learned a trade with His calloused hands, was hungry and thirsty, grew tired and slept, wept at the tomb of His friend Lazarus, felt anguish in Gethsemane, experienced abandonment on the cross. The Catechism says it firmly: "The Son of God (...) assumed a real and complete human nature (...) truly man, like us in all things but sin" (CCC 470–471).

This is the hypostatic union: two complete natures (divine and human) united in a single divine Person (the Person of the Son). Neither mixed nor separated. He is not "half God and half man," nor "God using a human body like a costume." Jesus is 100% God and 100% man, in every act, in every word, in every heartbeat. The divine does not annul the human; the human does not limit the divine (CCC 467–468).

Why it Matters that Jesus Is Man

The humanity of Jesus is not an accessory detail. It is essential for our salvation for at least three reasons:

First: to represent us. Only a human being could represent humanity before God. Jesus is the new Adam: where the first Adam disobeyed and dragged us into sin, the new Adam obeys unto death and restores us to grace (Rom 5:12–21).

Second: to understand us. As the Letter to the Hebrews says: "We do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who has been tested in every way as we are, yet without sin" (Heb 4:15). God doesn't understand us from the outside, as an external observer. He understands us from the inside, because He lived our life. When you pray tired, Jesus understands. When you weep a loss, Jesus understands. When you feel that God has abandoned you, Jesus understands (cf. Mk 15:34).

Third: to divinize us. St. Athanasius coined the boldest formula of the Church Fathers: "God became man so that man might become God." It doesn't mean we stop being creatures; it means that by grace we participate in the divine life itself. St. Peter says it this way: "He has granted to us his precious and very great promises, so that through them you may become partakers of the divine nature" (2 Pet 1:4). The Incarnation is not just the payment of a debt: it is the elevation of our nature to an infinite dignity (CCC 460).

Connection

This completely changes how we see our body, our work, our daily tiredness. If God became a carpenter, manual labor is holy. If God felt hunger, our bodily needs are not shameful. If God wept, our tears are not weakness: they reflect a God who has a human heart.

Ask yourself today: what part of your humanity (your body, your emotions, your tiredness, your limits) do you struggle to accept? Now look at Jesus: He accepted all of that voluntarily. He didn't wait for you to be perfect before loving you. He stepped into your concrete humanity, with everything that means. Your everyday life — with its routine, its effort, its hidden beauty — is the place where God chose to live thirty years before preaching three.

4. The Hidden Life: Thirty Years that Changed the World

One of the most perplexing facts in the Gospels is this: Jesus lived thirty years in silence before beginning His public ministry. Thirty years of which we know almost nothing, except that "he went down with them and came to Nazareth and was obedient to them" (Lk 2:51). The God of the universe, on His knees in a workshop, sanding wood, sweeping sawdust, obeying two human creatures.

Theology calls this the "hidden life" of Jesus (CCC 531–534), and it's one of the most revolutionary realities of our faith. It means that most of God's life on earth was ordinary. Anonymous. Silent. No miracles, no speeches, no crowds. Just work, family, prayer, and obedience. Nazareth teaches us that ordinary life — yours, mine — is not an obstacle to holiness: it is its raw material. God didn't need a spectacular stage to live thirty years of divine-human fullness. A workshop was enough.

Baptism, Temptation, and Transfiguration

When Jesus emerges from Nazareth, His public ministry opens with three scenes that condense His entire mystery:

Baptism in the Jordan: Jesus, sinless, gets into the line of sinners and descends into the water. It's an act of total solidarity: God immerses Himself where humans try to wash away their guilt. And in that moment, the heavens are torn open, the Spirit descends like a dove, and the Father's voice proclaims: "You are my beloved Son; with you I am well pleased" (Mk 1:11). It's the revelation of the Trinity: the Son in the water, the Spirit over Him, the Father from heaven (CCC 535–537).

Temptation in the Desert: forty days of fasting and spiritual combat. Satan offers Jesus three shortcuts: easy bread (turn stones into food), spectacle (throw yourself from the temple), and political power (all the kingdoms of the world). They are the same three temptations of every human being: reducing life to the material, manipulating God for our own ends, and worshipping power. Jesus responds with Scripture: "Man shall not live by bread alone," "You shall not put the Lord your God to the test," "You shall worship the Lord your God and him only shall you serve" (Mt 4:1–11). Where Adam fell in the garden, the new Adam conquers in the desert (CCC 538–540).

The Transfiguration: on Mount Tabor, Jesus allows His humanity to reveal for an instant the glory it concealed. His face shines like the sun, His garments become white as light, Moses and Elijah converse with Him about His "exodus" (the cross). Peter, dazzled, proposes building three tents and staying there. But the Transfiguration is not a destination: it's a preview. Glory passes through the cross. Tabor prepares Calvary (CCC 554–556).

Activity

Jesus spent thirty years in an absolutely normal life. Think about your typical week: the same routes, the same tasks, the same people. What would change if you lived that week knowing that God chose to live exactly like that for thirty years? Write down one concrete thing you would do differently.

5. Why Did God Become Man? The Four Reasons of the Catechism

The Catechism (CCC 456–460) unfolds four reasons why God became man. They are four dimensions of a single mystery, and each deserves reflection:

1. To save us by reconciling us with God (CCC 457). "God sent his Son (...) so that the world might be saved through him" (Jn 3:16–17). Salvation is not self-help. We cannot fix ourselves. We need a Savior. And God didn't send a third party: He came in Person.

2. So that we might know God's love (CCC 458). "In this the love of God was made manifest among us, that God sent his only Son into the world, so that we might live through him" (1 Jn 4:9). The Incarnation is not a theological argument: it is the most radical declaration of love that exists. God doesn't love us in word alone: He becomes one of us.

3. To be our model of holiness (CCC 459). Jesus Himself says: "Learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart" (Mt 11:29). He doesn't give us a manual; He gives us His life. Following Christ is not obeying a code: it's imitating a Person.

4. To make us partakers of the divine nature (CCC 460). This is the boldest reason. God doesn't just rescue us: He elevates us. The Incarnation doesn't end with Jesus; it extends through grace to every baptized person. As St. Irenaeus says: "The Word of God became what we are so that we might become what He is."

6. The Kenosis: God Emptied Himself to Love Us

St. Paul describes the mystery of the Incarnation with a surprising Greek word: kenosis, self-emptying.

"Though he was in the form of God, [he] did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross." (Phil 2:6–8)

God did not come in power and glory. He came in fragility. As a baby in a borrowed manger. As a carpenter in a disreputable village ("Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" Jn 1:46). As a condemned man facing the most humiliating death of the Empire. He emptied Himself not because He stopped being God — that is impossible — but because He chose not to cling to the privileges of His divinity. He hid His glory so we could bear it. He made Himself weak so the weak could approach God without fear (CCC 471–474).

The kenosis is the most radical expression of love. True love empties itself, gives itself, descends. It does not impose itself: it offers itself. It does not dominate: it serves. It does not demand: it gives. And precisely for that reason, "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow" (Phil 2:9–10). Exaltation comes after emptying. Glory passes through the cross. There is no shortcut.

The Annunciation: When God Asked for Permission

The exact moment of the Incarnation didn't happen in a temple or before a crowd. It happened in an insignificant village, in the silence of a room, between a Jewish teenager and an angel (Lk 1:26–38). And God, who could have taken our nature by force, asked for permission. He waited for Mary's "yes."

"The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy — the Son of God. (...) And Mary said: Behold, I am the handmaid of the Lord; let it be to me according to your word." (Lk 1:35, 38)

That "let it be" — Mary's fiat — changed the history of the universe. In that instant, the Word became flesh in her womb. The Second Person of the Trinity acquired a human heart that beat for the first time within a woman from Nazareth. The Virgin Mary is truly the Mother of God (Theotokos), as the Council of Ephesus (431) defined: because the One born of her was not an ordinary man to whom God was later united, but God Himself made man from the very first instant.

The virginal birth of Jesus (CCC 496–498, 502–507) is not a pious legend. It is a matter of faith, deeply significant: Jesus has no human father because His only Father is God. Mary conceived through the power of the Holy Spirit, without the agency of a man. This underscores that the initiative is entirely divine: salvation does not arise from human history; it breaks into it from above. And at the same time, it passes through human freedom: Mary's "yes" was real, free, and necessary.

Entry into Jerusalem: The King Not of This World

On Palm Sunday, Jesus enters Jerusalem mounted on a donkey, acclaimed by the crowd: "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord!" (Mk 11:9). The scene is a deliberate messianic declaration. But the King entering His city doesn't arrive on a chariot of war: He arrives on a beast of burden. He doesn't wield a sword: He is going to be pierced by a spear. He will reign not from a throne but from a cross, with a crown of thorns. "My kingdom is not of this world" (Jn 18:36). That week — Holy Week — condenses the entire mystery of His life: God made man is going to hand over His human life out of love for us.

Personal Reflection

If God emptied Himself out of love, what does that ask of us? The kenosis sets the pattern for Christian life. St. Paul says it immediately after the hymn: "Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus" (Phil 2:5). How do you live the kenosis in your daily life? Perhaps no one is asking you to die on a cross, but you are asked to die to your pride, your need for control, your impatience. To empty your ego so there's room for the other. To die a little each day so that Christ lives more in you. That is holiness: letting Christ's kenosis be prolonged in your flesh.

In what concrete area of your life are you being called to "empty" something today?

7. The Humanity of Christ: God with Our Face

One of the most persistent errors among Christians is to think of Jesus as God in disguise, merely appearing to be human. Nothing could be further from the truth. The Incarnate Word lived a humanity that was complete and real (CCC 470–478):

  • He was hungry after fasting in the desert (Mt 4:2).
  • He was thirsty at Jacob's well: "Give me a drink" (Jn 4:7).
  • He grew weary from the journey and sat down by the well (Jn 4:6).
  • He slept in the boat while the storm raged (Mt 8:24).
  • He wept at the tomb of Lazarus: "Jesus wept" (Jn 11:35, the shortest and most human verse in the Bible).
  • He was indignant at the merchants in the temple (Jn 2:15).
  • He rejoiced with His friends: He went to the wedding at Cana (Jn 2:1–11).
  • He felt fear in Gethsemane: "My soul is very sorrowful, even to death" (Mt 26:38).
  • He felt abandonment on the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Mt 27:46).
  • He had friends whom He loved in a special way: Mary, Martha, and Lazarus (Jn 11:5); Peter, James, and John; the beloved disciple.

Jesus didn't fake any of these things. He lived them truly, just as you do. That's why, when you approach God in prayer, you're not talking to a philosophical abstraction or a cosmic energy. You're talking to Someone who knows what it is to be tired, hungry, grieving, and feeling that heaven is silent. A God who understands you from the inside.

8. Jesus and the World: The Mother He Left Behind

One of the most beautiful pages in the history of salvation bears a distinctly American accent. In December 1531, barely ten years after the conquest, a "Lady from heaven" appeared to a recently converted indigenous man named Juan Diego on Tepeyac Hill, in what is now Mexico. The Virgin of Guadalupe didn't come as just another Marian apparition: she came as the Mother of Jesus, bringing her Son to a new continent.

Think of what this means: the same God who became man in Palestine chose a corner of the Americas to leave the only image of Himself not painted by human hands — an image of His Mother, miraculously imprinted on Juan Diego's tilma. Jesus gave us His Mother — "Behold, your mother" (Jn 19:27) — and she came to plant the faith on American soil. The God made man, the Incarnate Word, is not only the Jesus of Galilee: He is the Jesus of Guadalupe, the One who chose a humble people to carry His maternal love.

St. Juan Diego was canonized in 2002 by St. John Paul II, and his tilma remains intact, defying all scientific explanation, in the Basilica of Guadalupe — the most visited Marian shrine in the world. Five centuries later, the Virgin continues to tell us what she told Juan Diego: "Am I not here, I who am your Mother?" It is Christ who gave her to us, and it is Christ to whom she leads us.

For Reflection

1. Jesus asks you the same question He asked His disciples: "But you, who do you say that I am?" Don't answer with the catechism. Answer from your real life.

How would your daily life be different if you truly believed that Jesus is God?

2. Jesus experienced hunger, weariness, fear, and tears. How does it change your prayer to know that God understands your pain from the inside, not just from above?

Think of something concrete you're suffering right now. Imagine Jesus saying to you: "I went through that too."

3. The kenosis tells us that God emptied Himself out of love. In what concrete areas of your life do you need to "empty" your ego, your pride, your need for control?

Don't judge yourself. Identify just one area where you could take a small step this week.

4. Jesus spent thirty years in an absolutely ordinary life before beginning His ministry. God chose to live like you for most of His earthly life. What does this tell you about the value of your "normal" life?

How could you live your daily routine (work, study, family) differently if you believed that hidden within it is the stuff of holiness?

5. C.S. Lewis said: Jesus is either mad, a liar, or the Lord. There's no middle ground. Are you living as if Jesus is truly your Lord, or do you have Him tucked away in a "religious" zone of your life that doesn't touch your real decisions?

If Jesus is truly God, the logical consequence is not to admire Him: it is to follow Him with your whole life.

Key Idea

Jesus is neither half-God and half-man, nor God disguised as a human being. He is true God and true man: two complete natures in a single Person. And this mystery is not a theological curiosity: it is the most important news in history. Because it means that God does not save us from afar, but from within, sharing our flesh, our weariness, and our tears. "The Word became flesh": God pitched His tent among us. And that changes everything.

Cultural Riches

Film The Nativity Story (Catherine Hardwicke, 2006). One of the most beautiful and faithful portrayals of the Annunciation and the Birth of Jesus. The Annunciation scene — with Keisha Castle-Hughes as Mary — captures the wonder and the freedom of the fiat. The film was shot in Matera, Italy, and Morocco, with a realism that avoids sentimentality and kitsch.

Literature C.S. Lewis, The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe (The Chronicles of Narnia). Aslan, the great lion, is one of the most powerful Christ-figures in modern literature. It's not an exact allegory, but a "supposal": what would Christ be like if He became incarnate in a world of talking animals? The scene of the Stone Table (Aslan's sacrificial death) is one of the most effective catecheses on kenosis and atonement ever written for young people. Also Mere Christianity, chapter on the trilemma.

Art El Greco, The Disrobing of Christ (El Expolio, 1577–1579, Sacristy of Toledo Cathedral). Christ dressed in deep red, surrounded by a crowd that pushes and strips Him, yet with a serene gaze directed toward heaven. El Greco, a Cretan settled in Toledo, succeeded as few have in painting the dual nature of Christ: His tortured humanity and His serene divinity in a single canvas. Also the Byzantine Pantocrator icons: Christ as Lord of the universe, holding the Book of Life in one hand and blessing with the other — the oldest and most venerable image of Christ's divinity in Christian art.

Devotion Devotion to the Holy Name of Jesus has biblical roots (Phil 2:10) and was spread by St. Bernardine of Siena in the 15th century. The monogram IHS (abbreviation of IHSOUS, "Jesus" in Greek) became the emblem of the Jesuits. The feast of the Holy Name of Jesus is celebrated on January 3rd.

Culture St. Juan Diego and Our Lady of Guadalupe. Juan Diego's tilma — a garment of maguey fiber that would normally disintegrate in twenty years — remains intact five centuries later. The image has defied all scientific analysis: there are no brushstrokes, the fiber appears not to be painted as such, and in the eyes of the Virgin, thirteen microscopic human figures are reflected (discovered by Dr. José Aste Tonsmann in 1979). Jesus gave us His Mother, and she chose the Americas to plant her image.

Magisterium Redemptor Hominis (1979), the first encyclical of St. John Paul II. Its opening line is already famous: "The Redeemer of man, Jesus Christ, is the center of the universe and of history." And further on: "Christ reveals man to himself and discloses his sublime vocation" (RH 8, quoting Gaudium et Spes 22). It is fascinating reading for understanding why the Incarnation is the key to understanding not only God, but ourselves.

Music Handel's Messiah (1741): the "Hallelujah" chorus is the most famous musical celebration of Christ's divinity of all time. For contemporary worship, Hillsong's "What a Beautiful Name" captures the power of Jesus' name, and Matt Maher's "Lord, I Need You" expresses the personal reliance on Christ that flows from His dual nature.

Youth Catechism YouCat, questions 47–55 ("Is Jesus True God?" "Is Jesus True Man?" "What does it mean that Jesus is the Son of God?"). Each question includes a biblical quote, an icon, and references to the CCC. Available free at youcat.org and as an app.

Catechesis Real + True (osvnews.com/real-true), a multimedia project on the Catechism. The episode on the Incarnation is titled "God Became Man." Also the Ascension Presents channel with Fr. Mike Schmitz and the series The Chosen (Dallas Jenkins, 2019–present), which offers a dramatization of the Gospels that recovers the concrete, approachable humanity of Jesus in a way never seen before.
